item.title: Obsession, 2025 - ★★★★½
item.dc:creator: m
item.description: Well, this was incredibly intense to process, my heart is still racing, lol. it's rare to see a debut as confident and effective as this one. uncanny..., deeply unsettling, there where moments where i thought it couldn't get any better, and it managed to surpass them. I haven't felt this terrified by a movie in years.

item.title: The Age of Disclosure, 2025 - ★★
item.dc:creator: m
item.description: Never got to finish this last year. Decided to take the time now, since i just watched Disclosure Day anyway.

item.title: Disclosure Day, 2026 - ★★★★★
item.dc:creator: m
item.description:
I think there's a needful dialogue on how we treat blockbuster as pejorative on film discourse, as though scale and sincerity are somehow at odds with artistic value. If anything, the films that shaped my imagination most were the ones that embraced spectacle without embarrassment. I would not be who I am without E.T. or Jurassic Park. An affinity that explains my appreciation for this despite its obvious shortcomings.

I'd agreed with many critics that has argued that the screenplay stands as its weakest component. It does often feels disjointed. Characters drift in and out of narrative importance, thematic threads are introduced only to be partially abandoned, compromising every attempts of its own procedural ambitions. Yet, this works perfectly well in spite of those flaws. It's Spielberg operating in the register he's spent decades perfecting, those big awe-spectacle-full of sentiment and wonder. It's exactly give just a little bit of everything i need from a movie.

Perhaps that is Spielberg's special gifts—his understanding of cinema as a medium of amazement. This may not really be a good procedular thrilling movie. I can maybe find more intellectually science fiction or tight procedural thriller elsewhere. What I cannot find as easily is a filmmaker who still believes, without irony, that looking up at the unknown might change us. This one reminded me why I fell in love with movies in the first place.

item.title: Backrooms, 2026 - ★★★
item.dc:creator: m
item.description: There's a lot to admire about this as a debut feature, but there's also plenty of room for growth. I have to admit, the characters never quite worked for me. They're too shallow and often feel overly literal. The film also loses its footing once it runs out of ways to scares me with the endless maze. What's left isn't quite strong enough to sustain the sense of dread that makes the first half so effective.

item.title: Sleep No More, 2026 - ★★½
item.dc:creator: m
item.description:
Edwin’s most ambitious film yet, which is both a compliment and its undoing. There’s massive swing—from off-beat comedy, body horror, even cosmic dread, and social commentary—but, when you want to achieve something like this all at once, it’s usually ended up muddling the whole thing.
Formally it also hits the off-beat arthouse checklist perfectly: pulp visual, jaunty score, and dry feel-bad tragicomedy. But there’s this thing with a film with so much ambitions that what makes it less endearing despite the heights it tried to achieve, this one desperately lack irony. I think that’s the main element that makes this specific kind of surrealism work; sorry for citing another contemporary arthouse film who shared similarities, but for examples in A Useful Ghost or Chema García Ibarra’s The Sacred Spirit, an ironic detachment is used to ground the bizarre in the mundane, making the world feel lived-in. And we have very little of it here. Theres this weird pull I think between how the film wants to play around in the surreal world, and its sudden urgency to wrap things up like standard horror cinema. This culminates in a film that spends the runtime following the dynamics of the fourth of it’s character (including the factory owner), yet remains completely detached from them. I think if this either leaning much harder into those character dynamics, or just going totally off the rails into absurdity, would have done wonders. Shame tho because from what i’ve seen on screen, theres a stronger film in there somewhere.

PSA: I was completely fooled when Luthesa played the endearing character, and Rachel Amanda played the cynical one. Chuckled a bit from that. Great move. Edwin.

item.title: A South-facing House in Gyeonggi Province, 2024
item.ns0:filmTitle: A South-facing House in Gyeonggi Province
item.dc:creator: m

item.title: Levitating, 2026 - ★★★
item.dc:creator: m
item.description: Udah berusaha ngasih benefit of the doubt untuk gak mempersoalkan atau mendebat film ini dengan kacamata orientalis. But i couldn't help but notice: kenapa ya masih selalu main aman? Tau bahwa produser butuh nama besar untuk ngejual filmnya, tapi ada miss opportunity untuk bikin ini beneran indigenous cinema dengan ngga masukin pelaku seni-nya beneran atau mungkin aktor teater lokal jadi salah satu aktor utama di film-nya, itung-itung ngangkat nama baru ke industri. Secara visual, film wregas paling menyenangkan, banyak cut aneh dan juga movement kamera yang playful, but nothing really stood out. By his third feature, i'd expect him to move beyond what he did in his first two films, but it feels like he hasn't really progressed much at all.

item.title: The Drama, 2026 - ★★★★
item.dc:creator: m
item.description: Logged this while listening to Cameron Winter's 'Love Takes Miles.' Just thought I'd share.

item.description: Nothing is scarier than the mind filling in the blanks.

item.title: Ready or Not 2: Here I Come, 2026 - ★★★

item.dc:creator: m

item.title: Lee Cronin's The Mummy, 2026 - ★★
item.dc:creator: m
item.description: Suka kalo film horror emg asli jahanam, tapi ini gimana ya, lore drop-nya tipis banget. Eksposisinya malah kebanyakan makan durasi ketimbang ngasih bangun dunia-nya, either stick with being full evil, or give us some depth into the myth and lore. Padahal bisa dibuat lebih jahat dengan eksposisi yang serba nanggung itu. You can call this Evil Dead Rise: Cairo edition. Not enough mummy on this one man.

item.title: Ghost in the Cell, 2026 - ★★★
item.dc:creator: m
item.description:
At this point, ive just come to accept that walking into a Joko Anwar film means sitting through a bit of a sermon. I’ve had to tell myself to stop dare his film to explore beyond its initial premise and political cheap talks, mostly because the messaging usually ends up feeling a bit one-dimensional anyway, and no, it’s never wanted to be engaging in the first place. There are also some uncomfortable, queer-coded sexual assault jokes in this one that I’m pretty sure were thrown in for cheap comedic thrills rather than adding to the gritty reality of a prison ward (i'll wait the twitter thread to pop out later on this weekend).

But setting that misstep aside, this is honestly the most fun I’ve ever had with a Joko Anwar film. It’s highly ironic that this one delivered more laughs than actual comedies film (yes, Agak Laen 2). Kinda hilarious to leave it to a horror director to wrote some of the most entertaining and organic banter between his characters, indo comedy directors could actually take some notes.

On a final note: it’s also a bit ironic that the LSF decided to draw the censorship line at some male backside, but gave a total free pass to the fully fleshy, visceral gore in the movie. Really tell you the state of censor-hip (pun-intended) out here.

...plus 24 more. View the full list on Letterboxd.

item.title: 2024 End Year List: Documentary
item.dc:creator: m
item.description:
This year, I decided to separate fiction and documentary films in my end-of-year list. So much has happened in the world, and just ranking them as personal favorites felt wrong—it seemed to downplay the urgency of the issues we’re facing.

  • No Other Land

    There is no other documentary you need to watch more than this one. Against the backdrop of the ongoing Gaza genocide, No Other Land offers a searing portrayal of perpetual colonialism and the brutal realities faced by Palestinian villagers in Masafer Yatta. The film’s raw, unflinching narrative exposes the structural violence and relentless demolitions inflicted upon these communities while celebrating their unyielding spirit of resistance.

  • Soundtrack to a Coup d'Etat

    Through archival footage and an chaoting editing, Grimonprez navigates this complex episode of the start of Pax Americana order. His approach to storytelling mirrors the improvisational nature of jazz, where rhythms of tension and release echo the political upheavals of the time. Despite the intensity and fragmented nature of the visuals, Grimonprez ensures that the documentary never loses sight of its central message. The chaotic flow of images, sounds, and voices comes together in a way that distills the heart of this historical moment, making it comprehensible even to those unfamiliar with cold-war international politics.

  • Monisme

    I liked the post-colonial approach in Monisme, remind me much of Weerasethakul's Memoria (2021). What struck me most was how Rizaldi uses the figure of the mystic as a counter-narrative to the modern, scientific, rational Western worldview. Rizaldi doesn’t present this indigenous worldview in opposition to the scientist’s rationalism in a simplistic, binary way. Instead, both perspectives coexist within the film’s polyphonic structure, each presenting a legitimate form of knowledge, without privileging one over the other.

  • Dahomey

    As I wrote this excerpt, i realize the movies on my list are underlined an ongoing relevance of the post-colonial movement and its exploration of the lasting effects of colonialism. Each film, in its unique way, speaks to the complexities of power, identity, and on this, restitution,

    On Dahomey, Mati Diop brings a poetic sensibility to this weighty topic, elevating the narrative beyond legal or political debates into something far more human and profound. The objects being returned represent the stories, identities, and legacies of a people who were once colonized and stripped of their history.

  • The Gullspång Miracle

    Encapsulating the notion that life is stranger than fiction, it employs layered narratives that juggle the complexities of identity construction. Each twist and piece of information could becomes its own intriguing story. Maria Fredriksson’s direction has this uncanny ability to pull you into the mystery, and by the end, I couldn’t stop thinking about how fragile our understanding of “truth” really is. This is the kind of documentary that lingers in my mind long after the credits roll.

    Human stories, as this illustrates, can be both fascinating and terrifying.

  • Afternoons of Solitude
  • I'm Not Everything I Want to Be
  • Ryuichi Sakamoto | Opus
  • The Night Won't End
  • Trains

...plus 1 more. View the full list on Letterboxd.
